title: scope_globs reads security-controls.md from disk on every path-check call (N reads per Write/Edit hook)
evidence: _read_controls(root) opens and reads .codearbiter/security-controls.md on every call. scope_globs() calls _read_controls() unconditionally. path_in_globs() calls scope_globs(). In post-write-edit.py main(), is_ci_path(rel, root) and is_deploy_path(rel, root) are both called on every Write/Edit hook invocation (lines 123-130), each resolving through path_in_globs -> scope_globs -> _read_controls — two file reads per hook. When a commit triggers pre-bash.py, is_migration_path() adds a third call through the same chain. No cache exists between calls within a single hook invocation.
impact: 2–3 redundant disk reads of the same file per hook invocation, on the hottest user-interactive paths (every Write, Edit, Bash commit). On a spinning disk or network filesystem the compounded latency is directly felt on every tool call.
recommendation: Cache the controls text for the duration of a single process invocation. A module-level dict keyed by (root, mtime) — or even a simple module-level '_cached_controls' string set on first call — eliminates all redundant reads within a single hook process. Since each hook is a short-lived subprocess, a same-process cache is sufficient; no IPC needed.
acceptance_criteria: _read_controls returns the same string for the same (root, mtime) without re-opening the file
In post-write-edit.py, is_ci_path and is_deploy_path share one controls read per hook invocation
No regression in glob-match correctness tests

title: _glob_to_re recompiles all glob patterns to regex on every path_in_globs call
evidence: path_in_globs() calls scope_globs() which returns (includes, excludes) as plain lists of strings. path_in_globs() then calls _glob_to_re(g).match(rel) for every glob in both lists on every call (lines 302-304). _glob_to_re() builds a new re.compile() object each time. With MIGRATION_DEFAULT_GLOBS (5 entries), CI_DEFAULT_GLOBS (6 entries), and DEPLOY_DEFAULT_GLOBS (11 entries), a single post-write-edit.py invocation that hits all three checks compiles up to 44 regex objects per hook call. Python's re module has an internal LRU cache (re._cache, default size 512), but _glob_to_re builds the pattern string then immediately compiles it without using re.compile's caching path directly — the cache hit rate is accidental rather than designed.
impact: 44 regex compilations per Write/Edit hook invocation at steady state. regex compilation is CPU-bound and non-trivial for complex patterns. Across thousands of writes in a session this adds measurable latency.
recommendation: Pre-compile the default glob sets to regex at module load time (they are module-level constants). Store results as module-level tuples of compiled patterns. For the project-customised globs, cache the compiled set keyed by the controls mtime (same cache object as performance-001 recommendation). This reduces per-call regex work to zero for the default globs.
acceptance_criteria: MIGRATION_DEFAULT_GLOBS, CI_DEFAULT_GLOBS, DEPLOY_DEFAULT_GLOBS compiled patterns are module-level constants

title: project_root() spawns a git subprocess on every hook invocation with no caching
evidence: _hooklib.project_root() runs subprocess.run(['git','rev-parse','--show-toplevel'], timeout=5) unconditionally on every call. Every enforcement hook (pre-bash, pre-write, pre-edit, post-write-edit) calls project_root() as its first action in main(). Each hook is a separate subprocess invocation, so no process-level shared state helps — but within a single hook process, the call is made exactly once. The cost is therefore one git subprocess per hook call, not more. At ~5ms per git invocation on a local repo, this adds ~5ms to every Bash/Write/Edit tool call latency.
impact: ~5ms added latency per tool call from the git subprocess fork+exec. On Windows (where process creation is significantly slower than Linux) this is closer to 15-30ms. The hook fires on every Bash/PowerShell/Write/Edit — the most frequent tool calls.
recommendation: The project root can be derived without a subprocess when the working directory is inside a git repo: walk up from os.getcwd() looking for a .git directory (same approach statusline.py uses in its project_root). This is synchronous, O(depth), and avoids a process spawn entirely. Fall back to the subprocess for edge cases (git worktrees, bare repos). The statusline.py already has this pattern at lines 299-310.
acceptance_criteria: project_root() returns the same result as git rev-parse --show-toplevel for normal repos
No subprocess spawn for the common case (standard local git repo)
Worktree / bare repo case degrades gracefully to the existing subprocess path

title: statusline.py:session_start scans all ~/.claude/sessions/*.json files linearly on every render
evidence: session_start(sid) calls os.listdir(~/.claude/sessions), then for each .json file checks its size and reads+parses it looking for a sessionId match. The scan is linear over all session files with no index. This function is called on every statusline render (line 1146: 's0 = safe(session_start, sid)'). A long-running user with many prior sessions accumulates many .json files; the function reads and parses each one until it finds the match.
impact: O(N) file reads and JSON parses per statusline render where N = number of session metadata files. In practice N is usually small (tens), but the reads are sequential and synchronous on the critical render path. On Windows with slow process context switches even a dozen file opens is felt.
recommendation: The session metadata files are named by PID (line 669 comment: 'keyed by pid'). If the current PID is always the one we want, try os.path.join(d, f'{os.getpid()}.json') first. Alternatively, store the resolved start timestamp in the ledger on first find (the ledger is already read-modified-written per render) so subsequent renders skip the scan entirely.
acceptance_criteria: session_start returns correct value without scanning all session files on typical re-renders
Fallback to full scan when the fast path misses (new session / no cached value)

title: statusline.py:arbiter_state reads 4 .codearbiter/ files on every render with no caching
evidence: arbiter_state(root) reads: (1) CONTEXT.md for frontmatter (frontmatter() opens and reads the whole file), (2) overrides.log via count_matches() which opens and reads the whole file, (3) last-checkpoint via open(), (4) open-tasks.md via _read_board(). count_matches() is also called for open-questions.md (line 399), making it 5 file reads total per arbiter_state call. arbiter_state is called on every statusline render (line 1127: 'arb = safe(arbiter_state, root)'). None of these reads are cached across renders.
impact: 5 synchronous file reads per statusline render on the arbiter-enabled path. Statusline renders on every tool-call completion (StopHook). In a busy session with many tool calls this adds up to hundreds of redundant re-reads of files that rarely change between tool calls.
recommendation: Cache the arbiter state with an mtime-based invalidation: stat all 5 files, compute max(mtime), and return the cached dict if the max mtime is unchanged since the last call. A module-level variable suffices since statusline.py is a short-lived subprocess. Alternatively, cache just for the duration of a single render() call (a local variable passed down), which requires no persistence logic at all.
acceptance_criteria: arbiter_state returns cached result when none of the 5 input files changed
State is re-read correctly when any file is modified between renders

title: pre-bash.py:head_on_protected_tip spawns two additional git subprocesses unnecessarily in most invocations
evidence: head_on_protected_tip(cwd) is called when current_branch() returns empty (detached HEAD). It calls _rev(cwd, 'HEAD') then _rev(cwd, 'main') and _rev(cwd, 'master') — up to 3 subprocess.run calls in sequence. _rev() always spawns a new subprocess. The detached HEAD case is rare in practice (normal work is on a named branch), so these spawns occur almost never — but when they do trigger, 3 subprocess spawns are paid where 1 would suffice: git rev-parse HEAD main master in a single invocation returns all three in one process.
impact: 3 sequential subprocess spawns instead of 1 when in detached HEAD state on a commit attempt. Low frequency but easily avoidable.
recommendation: Combine the three rev-parse calls: ['git','rev-parse','HEAD','main','master'] returns three lines in one subprocess. Parse the three lines and compare. Eliminates 2 of the 3 subprocesses.
acceptance_criteria: Only 1 subprocess spawned instead of 3 in the detached HEAD path
